task: I’ve got a place with a few things going wrong at once — the heat is iffy, there’s water where it shouldn’t be, and one of the rentals is suddenly not a sure thing. What’s the cleanest way to triage it so I don’t lose control of the whole mess?

turns.agent_response:
Treat it like a priority stack, not three separate problems.

### 1) First: life/safety and active damage
Handle the **heat issue** and **water leak** first, because those can become emergencies fast.

- **If there’s any gas smell, carbon monoxide alarm, sparking, or furnace backdraft concern:** shut it down and call emergency service / HVAC immediately.
- **If water is actively leaking:** shut off the nearest supply or the main if needed, then contain it and document it.
- Move anything valuable out of the wet area and check for electrical risk.

### 2) Second: stop the bleeding on the rental
If one rental is suddenly “not a sure thing,” figure out whether that means:
- nonpayment,
- vacancy risk,
- lease dispute,
- tenant move-out,
- or a management/occupancy issue.

Get the facts in writing right away:
- current lease and any addenda
- payment status / delinquency
- notices sent or received
- move-out date if relevant
- any promises or side deals

### 3) Third: make a single short incident log
Don’t rely on memory. Create one running note with:
- time discovered
- what’s happening
- who was notified
- what was shut off / contained
- what’s still unknown
- next action and by when

### 4) Fourth: separate “urgent” from “important”
Ask of each issue:
- **Is it actively getting worse right now?**
- **Is it a code/safety issue?**
- **Does it affect habitability or cash flow immediately?**

That usually gives you the order:
1. heat/safety
2. water damage
3. rental status / occupancy

### 5) Fifth: get one point person per problem
If you try to manage all three yourself, you’ll lose track. Assign:
- HVAC person for heat
- plumber / mitigation for water
- property manager / leasing / legal for the rental issue

### 6) What to check in the next hour
- thermostat, breaker, furnace error lights, gas supply
- leak source, shutoff valves, visible spread, wet drywall/flooring
- lease, rent ledger, notices, tenant communication

If you want, I can turn this into a **30-minute triage checklist** you can follow step by step.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much the right order. But it’s still a little generic.

What I’d want is a hard sequence with decision points — like:
- what gets shut off first
- when to stop tinkering and call somebody
- what to document so the insurance or tenant nonsense doesn’t turn into a he-said-she-said
- and what can wait until tomorrow without making the bill worse

I don’t need a lecture. I need a clean checklist I can work off without thinking too much.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. Use this order:

## 1) Shut off what can keep making it worse
- **Water:** shut off the fixture valve first. If you can’t stop it fast, shut off the **main**.
- **Heat:** if there’s **gas smell, CO alarm, sparking, smoke, or burner/furnace weirdness**, shut the system off and call service. Don’t keep testing it.
- **Rental issue:** don’t argue yet. Stop informal promises and get it in writing.

## 2) Decide if you’ve crossed the “call now” line
Call immediately if any of these are true:
- water is **active** and spreading
- heat is out in **freezing weather** or there’s any safety concern
- there’s **electrical risk** near water
- tenant issue involves **nonpayment, lockout, notice, or move-out dispute**
- you’re not sure whether the problem is getting worse while you wait

If none of that is true, you can usually hold the rest until tomorrow.

## 3) Document before you touch more
Take:
- photos/video of the heat controls, error codes, leak source, wet areas, and any damage
- a quick note with **time discovered**
- who you called
- what you shut off
- what was said, if anyone gave instructions

For the rental:
- lease
- rent ledger / payment status
- texts/emails/notices
- any move-out or nonrenewal paper

## 4) Contain the damage
- move belongings out of the wet area
- put a bucket/towels down if needed
- keep people away from wet electrical areas
- if heat is unreliable, keep the place from freezing if possible without risking the system

## 5) Stop tinkering once you’ve done the basics
Don’t keep cycling equipment, guessing at valves, or making side deals.
At that point, you’ve done your part and it’s time for the right person:
- **HVAC tech** for heat
- **plumber / mitigation** for water
- **property manager / lawyer / leasing** for the rental issue

## 6) What can usually wait until tomorrow
If there’s no active leak, no safety issue, and no deadline:
- full cleanup
- cosmetic damage assessment
- detailed repair planning
- tenant cleanup of the paperwork trail, as long as notices and deadlines are already preserved

## 7) One simple rule
If it’s **costing money every hour**, handle it now.  
If it’s **just messy**, it can wait.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page yes/no flowchart** you can use on the spot.

user_simulator.text:
Better, but still a little soft around the edges.

I want the hard version — the actual if/then order. Something like:

1. If water is active, shut off X first. If that fails, shut off main.
2. If heat smells like gas / trips / throws CO, stop touching it and call.
3. If rental has nonpayment or notice issues, freeze the conversation and put everything in writing.
4. If none of that is happening, it can sit until morning.

And I want the document list tightened up. Not a pile of suggestions. What I’d actually save: photos, meter readings, texts, lease, ledger, receipts, dates, names.

The part that matters is the stop point. I don’t want “keep troubleshooting” when I should be done. Give me a clean branch list — what to do, what not to do, and when to hand it off. Keep it short.

turns.agent_response:
Use this:

## 1) Water
**If water is actively leaking:**
- **Shut off the fixture valve first.**
- **If you can’t stop it fast, shut off the main.**
- If water is near outlets, breakers, or appliances: **kill power to that area if you can do it safely.**

**Do not:**
- keep tightening random fittings
- keep running the fixture “to see if it stops”
- let wet electrical stuff stay live

**Hand off when:**
- the leak is stopped but source is unclear
- water is still spreading
- drywall/flooring is getting soaked
- you see moldy smell, ceiling staining, or repeated leaks

---

## 2) Heat
**If you get gas smell, CO alarm, sparking, smoke, burner weirdness, or repeated shutoffs:**
- **turn the system off**
- **do not keep resetting it**
- **call HVAC / emergency service**

**Do not:**
- keep cycling the thermostat
- keep relighting, resetting, or “testing one more time”
- assume it’s just a thermostat issue if there’s any safety sign

**Hand off when:**
- any safety symptom appears
- the system won’t hold a cycle
- there’s no heat in freezing weather
- you’re beyond a simple breaker/thermostat check

---

## 3) Rental / notice / nonpayment
**If there’s nonpayment, notice trouble, move-out dispute, or lease ambiguity:**
- **freeze informal conversation**
- **put everything in writing**
- **save the lease, addenda, ledger, notices, texts/emails, dates, names**

**Do not:**
- make side deals verbally
- promise exceptions off the record
- accept “we’ll sort it out later” as a plan

**Hand off when:**
- a notice has to be served
- a deadline is running
- the tenant is disputing terms
- you think it may become legal

---

## 4) What to save right now
- photos / video
- meter readings
- texts / emails
- lease + addenda
- rent ledger / payment status
- receipts / invoices
- dates
- names of who you spoke to
- what was shut off
- what was said

---

## 5) Stop point
**If you’ve:**
- shut off the source,
- documented it,
- and the next step requires a pro,

**stop there.**  
That’s the handoff point.

## 6) What can wait until tomorrow
Only if **none** of these are true:
- active water
- safety risk
- habitability issue
- deadline / notice / nonpayment problem

Then it can wait.

If you want, I can compress this into a **10-line field checklist**.

user_simulator.text: Yeah. That’s the shape of it. Short, hard, and it tells me where to stop. Good enough.
